title: AstraZeneca pens $2.1B Sino deal for challenger to Merck's COPD drug Ohtuvayre
summary: AstraZeneca has agreed to pay Sino Biopharmaceutical $200 million upfront plus up to $1.9 billion in achievable milestones for exclusive ex-China rights to TQC3721, a late-phase PDE3/4 inhibitor targeting chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Developed by Sino's Chia Tai Tianqing Pharmaceutical subsidiary, the nebulized formulation of TQC3721 has reached phase 3 in China, with a dry powder inhaler version in phase 2. The asset is positioned as a potential best-in-class challenger to Merck's Ohtuvayre (ensifentrine), which generates $131 million per quarter; head-to-head comparisons show TQC3721 achieved up to 147 mL peak FEV1 improvement in phase 2 versus Verona Pharma's reported 124 mL. The deal adds to AstraZeneca's broader COPD pipeline, including positive phase 3 data for anti-IL-33 antibody tozorakimab planned for regulatory submission. For investors, the $2.1B total deal value signals aggressive competition in the inhalation drug market following Merck's $10 billion acquisition of Verona Pharma.

# AstraZeneca pens $2.1B Sino deal for challenger to Merck’s COPD drug Ohtuvayre

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 8, 2026 5:00am

[AstraZeneca](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/astrazeneca) [Sino Biopharmaceutical](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/sino-biopharmaceutical) [Merck & Co.](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/merck-co) [Ohtuvayre](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/ohtuvayre)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astrazeneca-pays-sino-200m-challenger-mercks-copd-drug-ohtuvayre#)

AstraZeneca is paying Sino Biopharmaceutical $200 million upfront for ex-China rights to a late-phase challenger to Merck & Co.’s chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) drug Ohtuvayre.

The deal, which [includes](https://www1.hkexnews.hk/listedco/listconews/sehk/2026/0708/2026070800003.pdf) (PDF) up to $1.9 billion in milestones, covers a PDE3/4 inhibitor, TQC3721. Sino, working through its Chia Tai Tianqing Pharmaceutical subsidiary, has advanced a nebulized formulation of the drug candidate into phase 3 development in China. A dry powder inhaler formulation is in phase 2 and Sino has hailed TQC3721 as a potential best-in-class asset after [posting](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41812987/) mid-stage data on the candidate.

Merck’s Ohtuvayre has set the benchmark in the PDE3/4 class. When the U.S. pharma [acquired](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/merck-sweetens-its-portfolio-10b-acquisition-verona-and-potential-copd-blockbuster-ohtuvayre) Ohtuvayre last year in its $10 billion buyout of Verona Pharma, Merck CEO Robert Davis [said](https://onlinexperiences.com/scripts/Server.nxp?LASCmd=AI:4;F:QS!10100&ShowUUID=335C4D34-07B0-4915-B519-3B6265B5D62E&LangLocaleID=1033&Referrer=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.merck.com%2Finvestor-relations%2Fevents-and-presentations%2F) the asset has “multibillion-dollar peak commercial potential.” Ohtuvayre [generated](https://www.merck.com/news/merck-co-inc-rahway-n-j-usa-announces-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-highlights-significant-regulatory-approvals-and-clinical-milestones/) revenue of $131 million in the first quarter.

While Merck is racing to accelerate Ohtuvayre growth, AstraZeneca has spotted an opportunity to crash the PDE3/4 party. TQC3721 improved peak FEV1, a measure of lung function, by up to 147 mL at Week 4 of a phase 2 trial. For comparison, Verona [reported](https://www.veronapharma.com/news/verona-pharma-reports-positive-top-line-data-in-4-week-phase-2b-copd-study-with-nebulized-ensifentrine-on-top-of-tiotropium-therapy/) improvements of up to 124 mL in a phase 2 study.

Verona published its phase 2 data in 2020 and [won](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/fda-nod-veronas-ohtuvayre-set-jack-copd-market) FDA approval for Ohtuvayre in 2024. AstraZeneca can draw on its COPD experience and expertise as it works to close the gap on Ohtuvayre. The U.K.-based pharma sells Symbicort and Breztri in indications including COPD, [generating](https://www.astrazeneca.com/content/dam/az/PDF/2026/eq1/Q1-2026-results-announcement.pdf) (PDF) sales of $1.2 billion from the drugs in the first quarter.

TQC3721 will join a pipeline of next-generation COPD drug candidates in development at AstraZeneca. In [March](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astrazeneca-delivers-dream-result-phase-3-copd-hits-breathe-life-class-rocked-flops) and [April](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astrazenecas-faith-il-33-inhibitor-continues-pay-latest-copd-phase-3-win), the company reported positive phase 3 data on its anti-IL-33 antibody tozorakimab, which it plans to send to regulators. AstraZeneca is also [running](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT07082738) a phase 2 trial of the IRAK4 inhibitor, AZD6793, in COPD.

The Sino deal also gives AstraZeneca exclusive global rights to certain future development programs. The announcement came the same day that Sino said it had secured the China licenses to two of GSK's commercial-stage respiratory drugs—the triple therapy inhaler Trelegy ⁠Ellipta and the dual therapy inhaler ​Anoro Ellipta.

title: Boehringer bags PVT's viral vector platform for cancer vaccine R&D
summary: Boehringer Ingelheim has secured an exclusive license to Prime Vector Technologies' (PVT) Orf virus (ORFV) viral vector platform for next-generation cancer vaccine development, under terms featuring undisclosed upfront and milestone payments. PVT retains rights to pursue infectious diseases and other applications with the platform, which has already demonstrated clinical feasibility through its COVID-19 vaccine candidate Prime-2-CoV. ORFV, a parapoxvirus that infects sheep and goats, has been studied for over a decade as a potential cancer therapeutic due to its safety profile, support for repeat dosing, and immunogenic properties—though manufacturing scalability and human safety uncertainties have historically constrained development. The deal advances Boehringer's longstanding oncolytic virus strategy dating to 2016, including the VSV-GP (BI-1831169) program and its Austrian ViraTherapeutics facility.
analysis_notes: Decent article but relatively thin on concrete deal economics (no valuations disclosed). Priority is medium since Boehringer's oncolytic virus track record with VSV-GP has been mixed and the PVT platform has not yet entered clinical trials.

# Boehringer bags PVT’s viral vector platform for cancer vaccine R&D

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 7, 2026 10:35am

[Boehringer Ingelheim](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/boehringer-ingelheim) [viral vectors](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/viral-vectors) [oncolytic virus](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/oncolytic-virus) [cancer vaccine](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/cancer-vaccine)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/boehringer-bags-pvts-viral-vector-platform-cancer-vaccine-rd#)

Boehringer Ingelheim has landed an exclusive license to Prime Vector Technologies’ (PVT) viral vector platform for developing next-generation cancer vaccines.

The deal, which [features](https://cdn.website-editor.net/s/be9a4740c19f4a54933c082ad19f51b3/files/uploaded/260707+Press+Release.pdf?Expires=1786017581&Signature=khJuGknd-XSD9ZKF-7FS~6Ns2Nvj9dz90LHkRxzuBqTDJbMdleO4PaZCyhtZwSYq9wZOK1KiqCTd84go8n97U6CcrVuF1844lZsHieu4oA2PDSxF3ir5RsxejqnrVOfOzaI2v6BE7RGMPW60XX9cmIL-0JiCaSnyMlkcJHudmRiVlIWmWBTK3SkxFwasjs-5~9da7OkmPoDvzpETy~qLKZArNqctSjSPJodYrR6zxwx25bh4JQRN4XhF5uQvA7iERRLmDln9xmVj6Pw-Zyzf1zSf0bs0ABvOFB5-RvfNSqQLEeLeRDRTQQRcaHYVS8sTtj~QZ15OhaMvWO6oNEwpZg__&Key-Pair-Id=K2NXBXLF010TJW) (PDF) undisclosed upfront and milestone payments, covers the use of PVT’s Orf virus (ORFV) platform. PVT has studied the platform across cancer and infectious diseases, including through a COVID-19 program that reached the clinic. Boehringer has secured rights to use the platform for cancer vaccines, leaving PVT free to explore infectious diseases and other applications.

Interest in using ORFV, a parapoxvirus that infects sheep and goats, for cancer treatments dates back more than a decade. In 2012, researchers [called](https://www.cell.com/molecular-therapy-family/molecular-therapy/fulltext/S1525-0016(16)32538-2?_returnURL=https%3A%2F%2Flinkinghub.elsevier.com%2Fretrieve%2Fpii%2FS1525001616325382%3Fshowall%3Dtrue) ORFV “an ideal viral backbone for the development of a cancer therapeutic,” reflecting evidence that the virus is safe in humans, supports repeat dosing and triggers a potent immune response.

Yet other pathogens, such as herpes simplex virus (HSV), adenovirus and vaccinia virus, have played a bigger role in cancer drug development to date. Amgen’s Imlygic uses HSV, as does the Replimune asset that is weeks away from an FDA approval decision.

Manufacturing scalability and uncertainties regarding safety in humans have limited use of ORFV, PVT CEO Ralf Amann [said](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rmv.70038) in a paper published last year. Amann and his co-author cited Prime-2-CoV, PVT’s COVID-19 vaccine candidate, as a program that has cleared the uncertainties. The COVID-19 program [generated](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/12/11/1288) feasibility, safety and immunogenicity evidence and established a manufacturing process.

PVT’s cancer vaccine work has yet to reach the clinic. But Boehringer has seen enough promise to write a check, securing a chance to assess the platform as a standalone cancer vaccine and in combination with other pipeline programs.

The deal furthers Boehringer’s longstanding interest in oncolytic viruses. In 2016, the German drugmaker [secured](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/boehringer-nabs-up-to-eu210m-oncolytic-virus-option-deal-austrian-startup) an option to buy ViraTherapeutics. Boehringer [exercised](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/boehringer-buys-oncolytic-virus-startup-viratherapeutics) its option in 2018 and [began](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05155332?tab=study) a phase 1 trial of VSV-GP, also called BI 1831169, in 2022. The company [reported](https://www.annalsofoncology.org/article/S0923-7534(24)02605-X/fulltext) initial data in 2024. Boehringer continues to list VSV-GP in its clinical pipeline but recently suspended the phase 1 trial.

Boehringer kept ViraTherapeutics’ site in Austria after buying the biotech. In January, ViraTherapeutics [unveiled](https://www.viratherapeutics.com/media-latest-news#20260601) a facility expansion intended to support an expansion beyond the roughly 45 people that made up its team at that time.

title: FDA Halts Release of New Drug Rejection Letters While Working to Formalize Policy
summary: The FDA has temporarily paused its controversial policy of publishing Complete Response Letters (CRLs) in real-time after an unnamed pharmaceutical company filed a citizen petition through Covington & Burling, arguing the letters exposed proprietary and unredacted confidential information. The agency originally announced the transparency program in September under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s radical transparency initiative and FDA Commissioner Marty Makary; the 89-letter database release following 200 prior CRLs included high-profile rejections from Ultragenyx, Capricor Therapeutics, and Replimune. The June 27 pause applies retroactively from April, leaving more recent drug denials like Sobi's gout drug without published CRLs despite prior precedent of full transparency. Meanwhile, the FDA has proposed a rule (RIN 0910-AJ16) that would expand the commissioner's authority to release CRLs by eliminating the presumption that marketing applications constitute confidential commercial information—legislative work Makary pursued before departing the agency in May. This pause represents a significant operational policy shift with material implications for how biotech and pharma companies navigate the regulatory submission process going forward.

# FDA halts release of new drug rejection letters while working to formalize policy

By
[Darren Incorvaia](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/darren-incorvaia) Jul 8, 2026 1:33pm

[Food and Drug Administration (FDA)](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/food-and-drug-administration-fda) [Complete Response Letter](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/complete-response-letter) [FDA rejection](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/fda-rejection) [Marty Makary](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/marty-makary)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-halts-release-new-drug-rejection-letters-while-working-formalize-policy#)

The FDA has paused a controversial policy of releasing letters describing its rationale for rejecting drugs, Fierce Biotech has learned, following a citizen’s petition from an unnamed pharma company in April.

The agency announced a plan last September to publish complete response letters (CRLs) [in real-time](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-announces-plan-publish-drug-rejection-letters-real-time-releases-new-batch-including) when making decisions to approve or reject drug applications. But after a petition challenged the policy for its potential to publicize proprietary information, the FDA “temporarily paused” the effort starting in April, a Department of Health and Human Services spokesperson told Fierce.

“The agency is evaluating the process and potential next steps,” the spokesperson added.

The most recent rejection added to the FDA’s [database](https://open.fda.gov/crltable/) is the April 22 spurning of AbbVie’s investigational botulin toxin trenibotulinumtoxinE. More recent rejections, like the late June denial of a [gout prospect](https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/fda-rejects-sobi-gout-drug-over-manufacturing-issues-sparing-amgen-blockbuster) from Sobi, have not had CRLs published.

The CRL policy was part of a “radical transparency” push championed by Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., and former FDA Commissioner Marty Makary, M.D. The September announcement was accompanied by a tranche of 89 letters that followed an [earlier drop](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-releases-initial-batch-200-drug-rejection-letters) of 200 CRLs last July.

That fresh batch of 89 letters included recent high-profile rejections like Ultragenyx’s [gene therapy](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/ultragenyx-takes-another-hit-fda-snubs-aav-gene-therapy-over-manufacturing-issues) for Sanfilippo syndrome type A, Capricor Therapeutics’ Duchenne muscular dystrophy [cell therapy](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-rejects-capricors-dmd-cell-therapy-asks-more-data) and Replimune’s [melanoma drug](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/replimunes-request-melanoma-approval-rejected-fda-cratering-stock) RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec).

In challenging the policy, law firm Covington & Burling wrote on behalf of the undisclosed pharma company that the agency needed to involve companies more in the disclosure process and be sure to fully redact all confidential information. The petition listed several specific incidences of unredacted material information in published CRLs, including rejections for [Lykos Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/several-concerning-observations-fda-sheds-more-light-rejected-drugs), [Stealth BioTherapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/hanging-our-fingernails-stealth-ceo-questions-efficiency-behind-fda-rejection-rare-disease) and others.

When its CRL was published, Capricor released a statement of its own saying it had not been notified in advance by the FDA, further accusing the agency of [misinterpreting](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/capricor-shares-response-cell-therapy-denial-after-fda-publicly-releases-rejection-letter) its efficacy data.

Though the policy has been paused for now, the FDA has launched efforts to codify it. The regulator has [proposed a rule](https://www.reginfo.gov/public/do/eAgendaViewRule?pubId=202510&RIN=0910-AJ16) that would expand the commissioner’s ability to release CRLs and “eliminate the longstanding presumption that the mere existence of a marketing application constitutes confidential commercial information.”

In April, former FDA leader Makary also asked Congress to amend the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to explicitly allow the agency to release information contained in CRLs, part of a [suite of asks](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/year-2-makarys-fda-has-plenty-requests-congress-including-major-new-pathway-and-new) Makary made to lawmakers. Makary [departed](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/fda-commissioner-marty-makary-resigns-capping-turbulent-tenure) the FDA in May and has been replaced on an acting basis by Kyle Diamantas.

title: Fierce Biotech Fundraising Tracker '26: Celea raises $180M for phase 3 study of PureTech IPF drug
summary: Fierce Biotech's annual Fundraising Tracker compiles biopharma venture capital deals of $50 million and above from Jan–Jul 2026. The July highlight features London-listed Celea Therapeutics, a PureTech spin-out, raising $180M in debut financing for its antifibrotic deupirfenidone to advance an IPF head-to-head phase 3 study against pirfenidone. The tracker catalogues ~40 rounds across multiple sectors: Ollin Biosciences ($330M Series B for VEGF/Ang2 bispecific OLN324), Isomorphic Labs ($2.1B Series B for AI drug discovery), NewLimit ($435M Series C for longevity/fatty liver assets), Beeline Medicines ($300M from Bain for autoimmune programs from BMS), and Parabilis/Corsera Health/Alveus among many others. Notable themes include AI-driven drug discovery, in vivo CAR-T, GLP-1/obesity, anti-amyloid antibodies, RNAi therapeutics, gene editing, ADCs, and male contraceptives—reflecting a market still flush with capital across diverse modalities.
analysis_notes: Tracker article—contains no single narrative story. Covers ~40 separate funding rounds with only brief summaries each (3–5 sentences per company). Most useful as a market-scanning reference for investors tracking VC flow and modality trends rather than a deep-dive on any one deal. Celea is the July headline item; the highest individual round is Isomorphic Labs at $2.1B (May). Priority is medium: valuable context but low depth per company.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Fierce Biotech Fundraising Tracker '26: Celea raises $180M for phase 3 study of PureTech IPF drug

By
[Will Maddox,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) [Darren Incorvaia,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/darren-incorvaia) [James Waldron,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron) [Gabrielle Masson](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/gabrielle-masson-0) Jul 6, 2026 5:00am

[biotech venture capital](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-venture-capital) [venture capital (VC)](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/venture-capital-vc) [Venture Capital](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/venture-capital) [Fundraising](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/fundraising)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-fundraising-tracker-26#)

A new year brings a fresh Fierce Biotech Fundraising Tracker designed to record the significant amount of venture capital that flows into biopharma.

We’re keeping the same guidelines used for [last year’s tracker](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-fundraising-tracker-25) and will only include financing rounds of $50 million and above.

## July

**July 2—Celea Therapeutics**

**Series:**“Debut financing”

**Amount:** $180M

**Investors:** RA Capital Management, Leaps by Bayer, PureTech Health, a “large, U.S.-based healthcare-focused fund” and a “leading sovereign wealth fund”

Almost a year after London-listed Celea was founded by PureTech to take forward its promising idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis candidate deupirfenidone, the hub-and-spoke biotherapeutics company has touted what it describes as one of the largest debut financings of 2026 for its spin-out. The cash will be used to take the antifibrotic into a head-to-head phase 3 study against the commonly used IPF drug pirfenidone, which is pencilled in for the third quarter. While approved antifibrotics for IPF slow disease progression, PureTech and Celea hope that deupirfenidone will continue to demonstrate the potential to stabilize lung function decline to levels comparable to normal aging. [Release](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260701866873/en/Celea-Therapeutics-Announces-%24180-Million-Financing-to-Advance-Deupirfenidone-as-a-Potential-New-Standard-of-Care-to-Treat-Idiopathic-Pulmonary-Fibrosis-IPF)

## June

**June 25—Lycia Therapeutics**

**Series:** D

**Amount:** $75M

**Investors:** Janus Henderson Investors, Balyasny Asset Management, Adage Capital Management, HBM Healthcare Investments, OrbiMed, Eli Lilly, Franklin Templeton, Invus, RTW Investments and Venrock Healthcare Capital Partners.

Autoimmune, inflammatory and allergic disease biotech Lycia has pulled down an oversubscribed series D from a suite of big-name investors, including Eli Lilly. The biotech plans to use the funds to support LCA-0061, which is designed to deplete immunoglobulin E for allergic diseases, and LCA-0321, a LYTAC degrader designed to selectively deplete thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor autoantibodies for Graves’ disease. Alongside the news of the fundraise, Lycia said it brought on Amy Bachrodt as chief financial officer and promoted Karen Flick to general counsel. [Release](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/06/25/3317430/0/en/lycia-therapeutics-raises-75-million-in-oversubscribed-financing-and-strengthens-leadership-team-with-appointment-of-chief-financial-officer-and-general-counsel.html)

**June 25—Oblenio Bio**

**Series:** B

**Amount:** $62M

**Investors:** Pfizer Ventures, Deep Track Capital, GV and Aditum Bio.

After its [founding](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/vc-firm-aditum-bio-hatches-autoimmune-focused-oblenio-bio-help-chinese-biotech) two years ago, Oblenio Bio has completed an oversubscribed series B fundraise meant to push its trispecific T-cell engager, LBL‑051, into clinical testing. The candidate is licensed from China's Leads Biolabs and works by targeting BCMA, CD19 and CD3. The company didn't disclose the indication it plans to pursue in testing, only noting that the drug is "designed to reset the immune system in patients with severe autoimmune diseases." [Release](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/vc-firm-aditum-bio-hatches-autoimmune-focused-oblenio-bio-help-chinese-biotech)

**June 24—Ollin Biosciences**

**Series:** B

**Amount:** $330M

**Investors:** TCGX, ARCH Venture Partners, a16z Bio+Health, Blackstone Multi-Asset Investing, Commodore Capital, Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments), RA Capital Management, accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Investment Management, Inc., a leading sovereign wealth fund, Mubadala Capital and Monograph Capital.

Half a year after toppling Genentech’s eye medicine giant Vabysmo in an early trial, Texas biotech Ollin Biosciences has lassoed a $330 million series B to push its winning contender through a multi-trial phase 3 gauntlet. The company plans to launch its VEGF/Ang2 bispecific antibody OLN324 into at least three phase 3 trials. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/ollin-hauls-330m-enlist-vabysmo-challenger-phase-3-eye-disease-gauntlet)

**June 24—RQ Bio**

**Series:** A

**Amount:** $115M

**Investors:** Frazier Life Sciences, EQT Life Sciences, Forbion, Monograph, Wellington Management, LifeArc Ventures, Oxford Science Enterprises and the University of Oxford.

After its founding several years agoby U.K. scientists and LifeArc Ventures, RQ Bio is making a splash with a $115 million series A. The U.K. biotech plans to use the funds to propel its lead program, RQB01, into clinical testing next year. The candidate is a long-acting antiviral for flu prevention featuring characteristics that RQ believes could improve upon the status quo for vulnerable individuals. The company also plans to use the money to “advance our proprietary antibody discovery approach towards a pipeline of assets for prophylaxis of respiratory viral diseases.” [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/rq-bio-reels-115m-take-long-acting-flu-prevention-antiviral-clinic)

**June 23—Osanni Bio**

**Series:** B

**Amount:** $190M

**Investors:** Patient Square Capital, Horowitz Group, Invus Opportunities and the Retinal Degeneration Fund.

Osanni launched three years ago with a $27 million series A and has kept purposefully quiet ever since. The new raise will power clinical development of a lead candidate for dry age-related macular degeneration and also help propel six other preclinical assets into the clinic. Originally conceived with an eye disease focus, the biotech has since expanded to seven pipeline programs across ophthalmology, cardiology and cancer. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/discreet-biotech-osanni-bio-starts-making-noise-190m-series-b-clinical-push)

**June 23—Serapha Bio**

**Series:** A

**Amount:** $230M

**Investors:** RA Capital Management, RTW Investments, Janus Henderson Investors, Decheng Capital, Vivo Capital, Casdin Capital, LifeSci Venture Partners, Logos Capital, Balyasny Asset Management and Eventide Asset Management.

title: Fierce Biotech Layoff Tracker 2026: Novartis loses 322 US staffers; BioCryst ends internal programs
summary: Fierce Biotech's fifth annual layoff tracker compiles workforce reductions across the biopharma industry from January through July 2026, covering 80+ companies and highlighting the sector's accelerating contraction. The most consequential entries include Novartis cutting 472 total US positions across two separate rounds (322 in July plus 150 earlier), BioCryst shuttering all internal discovery programs and its Birmingham lab to pivot toward partnership-driven innovation, Biogen dismantling most of Apellis' research pipeline after its $5.6B acquisition, and ADC Therapeutics laying off 17% of its workforce following a safety scare with Zynlonta in a confirmatory trial. The tracker also documents several shutdowns—including IO Biotech's complete closure, f5 Therapeutics after six years, EveryOne Medicines closing before FDA guidance cleared, and Nido Biosciences shutting down post-Phase 2 failure—alongside major restructuring at Takeda (4,500 roles), BioNTech (1,860 manufacturing jobs), and Gilead/Arcellx ($7.8 billion acquisition integration). Clinical failures drove multiple layoffs: Neumora's navacaprant Phase 3 misses, Fulcrum scrapping its sickle-cell program after FDA pushback, Replimune's second melanoma rejection, and Urogenyx's dual brittle-bone study failures collectively triggered workforces cut across the mid-cap biotech segment.
analysis_notes: This is a meta-tracker article, not a single story. It aggregates 80+ layoff events across H1-H2 2026 into one long-form piece. The most analytically dense content appears in the July and June sections (Novartis 322 cuts + BioCryst program shutdown); January/February entries are listed with minimal detail. For investment decisions, operator teams should focus on the narrative entries rather than scanning all 80+. Notable signal: multiple companies shut down entirely (IO Biotech, f5 Therapeutics, EveryOne Medicines, Nido Biosciences), suggesting a broader wave of early-stage biotech failures alongside large-pharma cost cutting.

# Fierce Biotech Layoff Tracker 2026: Novartis loses 322 US staffers; BioCryst ends internal programs

By
[Gabrielle Masson,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/gabrielle-masson-0) [James Waldron,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron) [Will Maddox,](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) [Darren Incorvaia](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/darren-incorvaia) Jul 8, 2026 7:30am

[biotech layoffs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-layoffs) [layoffs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/layoffs) [Big Pharma](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/big-pharma) [Restructure](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/restructure)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

Welcome to Fierce Biotech’s fifth annual layoff tracker, a resource designed to keep pace with the sector’s steady stream of workforce reductions.

In 2025, industry layoffs continued to rise year over year, prompting the need for another edition of this article.

As always, please reach out to the Fierce Biotech editorial team with any news of a biopharma workforce reduction. We are committed to protecting the anonymity of all tips, but we do conduct further reviews to verify all information we receive.

_Editor’s note about the numbers:_ Layoff rounds that are part of a company’s larger, already-announced reorganization are not included in the monthly total count.

### July—1 company

**July 7 - Novartis.** The latest round of layoffs at the Swiss pharma's U.S. headquarters in East Hanover, New Jersey sees 322 pink slips sent out, mainly affecting the field sales, patient support and marketing organizations. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/novartis-dismisses-322-more-its-us-headquarters-nj)

**July 6 - Genentech.** A month after confirming the restructuring of its early development group (see June entry below), the Roche subsidiary has now disclosed that this change has formed part of a round of 103 layoffs.
